Back: Consistent hashing maps keys and nodes onto a ring so adding or removing nodes moves only a subset of keys.
Why: That minimizes rebalancing cost and supports scalable partitioned systems and caches.

Back: A quorum read requires enough replica responses (typically a majority) to satisfy a consistency threshold.
Why: Combined with write quorum settings, it helps bound stale reads in replicated data stores.

Back: Raft is a consensus algorithm that maintains a replicated log through leader election, log replication, and committed entries.
Why: It is widely used to keep cluster state consistent despite node failures and network issues.

Back: Two-phase commit coordinates distributed transactions with a prepare phase and a commit/abort phase.
Why: It enforces atomicity across participants but introduces blocking and coordination overhead.

Back: 2PC can block when the coordinator fails after participants prepare but before a final decision is known.
Why: That coordination bottleneck hurts availability and latency, so many systems prefer eventually consistent patterns.

Back: A Saga breaks a distributed transaction into local transactions linked by compensating actions on failure.
Why: It improves availability and scalability compared with global locking approaches like 2PC.

Back: A Bloom filter is a probabilistic set-membership structure that can return false positives but never false negatives.
Why: It is useful for quickly filtering impossible lookups before hitting slower storage layers.

Back: An LSM tree batches writes in memory and periodically compacts sorted immutable files on disk.
Why: This write-optimized design powers many NoSQL engines handling high-ingest workloads.

Back: A cache stampede occurs when many requests simultaneously miss or expire the same key and all rebuild it at once.
Why: Mitigations like request coalescing, jittered TTLs, and stale-while-revalidate prevent backend overload.

Back: TCP is a connection-oriented transport protocol that provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked byte delivery.
Why: Many higher-level protocols depend on TCP's reliability guarantees for correct end-to-end behavior.

Back: HTTP/2 adds multiplexing, header compression, and stream prioritization over a single connection.
Why: These features reduce latency and improve throughput for modern web applications.

Back: Sticky sessions route a user's requests to the same backend instance over time.
Why: They can simplify stateful apps short term but reduce balancing flexibility and resilience.

Back: In stateless architecture, servers do not store per-client session state between requests.
Why: That allows horizontal scaling and failover because any node can serve the next request.

Back: The bulkhead pattern isolates resources so one failing subsystem cannot exhaust the whole application.
Why: Isolation limits blast radius and prevents cascading outages during dependency failures.

Back: The heap is the JVM memory region where objects are allocated and reclaimed by garbage collection.
Why: Heap sizing and GC behavior directly affect latency, throughput, and out-of-memory risk.
